summary: Gruber examines Apple's Section 3.3.1 restrictions through the lens of middleware dependencies, using Miguel de Icaza's defense of MonoTouch as a jumping-off point. He acknowledges that MonoTouch is a 'lighter shade of gray' compared to Flash since it maps 1:1 to Apple's APIs rather than abstracting them away, but argues Apple's concern is about long-term platform control. He draws a historical parallel to Metrowerks PowerPlant, a popular Mac framework that became an anchor slowing Apple's transition to Mac OS X. While granting that developers like de Icaza have a reasonable position — that middleware risk should be theirs to take — Gruber frames Apple's stance as a preemptive bet: ban middleware now while they have the clout, rather than wait until entrenched dependencies make it impossible. He concludes, citing John Siracusa, that Apple is wagering its mobile future on maintaining absolute control over platform advancement.
key_points: MonoTouch differs from Flash's cross-compiler by mapping directly to Apple's native APIs rather than providing a cross-platform abstraction, but it's still a middleware layer Apple wants to prevent
Apple's PowerPlant experience — where a popular third-party framework became an anchor during the Mac OS X transition — informs their hostility toward middleware dependencies
The timing argument is key: Apple can ban middleware by fiat now while it has clout, but couldn't do so later if middleware became deeply entrenched
De Icaza's counter-argument that developers should bear their own middleware risk and let market competition sort out quality is acknowledged as reasonable
Apple's Section 3.3.1 is fundamentally a preemptive wager that controlling the developer platform end-to-end will preserve their technical lead
money_quotes: MonoTouch is not in the same boat as Flash's iPhone compiler, as it's not a cross-platform framework. It's a lighter shade of gray, if you will. But it's still a layer of middleware between developers and Apple's own APIs.
What happens three, four, five years from now if MonoTouch stops keeping up and hundreds (or thousands) of popular iPhone OS apps are dependent upon it? That's the scenario Apple wants to avoid.
Because PowerPlant (a) was popular and (b) didn't keep up with the latest platform advances in Mac OS X, it became an anchor attached to Apple, which slowed them down.

Apple learned that there were significant risks to letting any technology get between Apple's APIs and third-party developers. Apple couldn't fix PowerPlant. They couldn't port it to Mac OS X themselves. It was out of their hands.
Apple can ban them by fiat now; they can't ban them by fiat in a future where they're in widespread use.
With middleware layers, the underlying platform can get stuck with the way things are.
I don't know that Apple is right. As I said above, de Icaza's perspective — that these decisions and risks should be up to individual developers to make — is utterly sensible.
key_insight: Apple's middleware ban is a preemptive power play — easier to forbid third-party platform dependencies now than to untangle them later when the clout has shifted to developers.
